perturbation.original_text: (g) Confidential Information. The term "Confidential Information' means any and all data and information relating to the business conducted by the Company (whether constituting a Trade Secret or not) which is or has been disclosed to Master Distributor or of which Master Distributor became aware as a consequence of or through his relationship with Company and which has value to the Company and is not generally known by its competitors; provided, however, no information will be deemed "confidential" unless such information is treated by the Company as confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: (g) Confidential Information. The term "Confidential Information' means any and all data and information relating to the business conducted by the Company (whether constituting a Trade Secret or not) which is or has been disclosed to Master Distributor or of which Master Distributor became aware as a consequence of or through his relationship with Company and which has value to the Company and is not generally known by its competitors; provided, however, no information will be deemed "confidential" unless such information is treated by the Company as confidential, unless a court of competent jurisdiction deems it so.
perturbation.explanation: The change adds a condition that only a court can deem information confidential, contradicting the initial definition that relies on the Company's treatment of information. This creates ambiguity and potential conflict with trade secret protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the South Carolina Trade Secrets Act, which defines trade secrets based on factors like independent economic value and reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, not solely on a court's determination. The law states that it should not be generally known nor readily ascertainable by proper means. The modification creates an unnecessary dependency on court rulings.

Short title. This chapter may be cited as the "South Carolina Trade Secrets Act". HISTORY: 1992 Act No. 437, Section 3, eff June 15, 1992; 1997 Act No. 38, Section 1, eff May 21, 1997. Effect of Amendment The 1997 amendment rewrote this section. SECTION 39-8-20. Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Improper means" include theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, duties imposed by the common law, statute, contract, license, protective order, or other court or administrative order, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person by improper means; (b) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (c) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired by mistake or under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Owner" means the person or entity in whom or in which rightful legal or equitable title to the trade secret is reposed. (4) "Person" means an individual, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a condition requiring a court to deem information confidential, which contradicts the standard practice of companies defining and treating information as confidential for trade secret protection, as indicated by the Law Explanation. This misalignment with established trade secret practices potentially weakens the company's ability to protect its confidential information as trade secrets. The South Carolina Trade Secrets Act defines 'misappropriation' and 'improper means' but does not specify that a court must determine confidentiality; it implies that reasonable efforts by the owner to maintain secrecy are crucial. The change adds ambiguity, potentially creating a loophole where information treated as confidential by the company may not be protected until a court ruling, undermining the company's internal confidentiality measures.

perturbation.original_text: (q) Trade Secrets. The term "Trade Secrets" means the whole or any portion or phase of any data or information developed, owned, or licensed from a third party by the Company, including any formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, improvement, or process that: (i) derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use, and (ii) is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy.
perturbation.changed_text: (q) Trade Secrets. The term "Trade Secrets" means the whole or any portion or phase of any data or information developed, owned, or licensed from a third party by the Company, including any formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, improvement, or process that: (i) derives independent economic value, actual or potential, irrespective of whether it is generally known, and (ii) may or may not be the subject of efforts to maintain its secrecy.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the requirements that a trade secret must not be generally known and that reasonable efforts must be made to maintain its secrecy, directly contradicting the statutory definition.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text directly contradicts the definition of a trade secret under the South Carolina Trade Secrets Act (SCTSA). The SCTSA explicitly requires that a trade secret derives independent economic value from not being generally known and being the subject of reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. By removing these requirements, the contract's definition is no longer aligned with the legal standard, making it unenforceable under the SCTSA.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts the South Carolina Trade Secrets Act (SCTSA). The original text aligns with the generally accepted definition of 'Trade Secrets,' requiring that the information (i) derives independent economic value from not being generally known and (ii) is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. The changed text removes both of these requirements, stating that the information derives economic value irrespective of whether it is generally known, and may or may not be the subject of efforts to maintain its secrecy. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Trade Secrets' within the context of the 'South Carolina Trade Secrets Act' (SECTION 39-8-10). By removing the necessity for secrecy and efforts to maintain it, the modified text deviates significantly from the legal definition implied by the SCTSA, rendering it unenforceable under the law.

perturbation.original_text: 16. Governing Law, Forum for Litigation. This Agreement shall be interpreted and governed by the laws of the State of South Carolina, without giving effect to its conflicts of laws rules. Notwithstanding alternative dispute settlement provisions in the Master Distributorship Agreement, any action or proceeding to enforce or interpret this Agreement shall be brought only in the state or federal courts sitting in Charleston County, South Carolina, United States, and Master Distributor hereby irrevocably submits and consents to such exclusive jurisdiction and venue.
perturbation.changed_text: 16. Governing Law, Forum for Litigation. This Agreement shall be interpreted and governed by the laws of the State of South Carolina, but the laws of Texas will be used regarding trade secret ownership. Notwithstanding alternative dispute settlement provisions in the Master Distributorship Agreement, any action or proceeding to enforce or interpret this Agreement shall be brought only in the state or federal courts sitting in Charleston County, South Carolina, United States, and Master Distributor hereby irrevocably submits and consents to such exclusive jurisdiction and venue.
perturbation.explanation: By specifying that Texas law governs trade secret ownership, the contract introduces a conflict of laws issue, especially since the Master Distributor is organized under Texas law. This contradicts the initial provision stating that South Carolina law governs the entire agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South Carolina Choice of Law Rules
perturbation.law_explanation: While South Carolina generally respects choice-of-law provisions, applying Texas law specifically to trade secret ownership creates uncertainty and potential conflict. A court would need to determine whether this specific carve-out violates public policy or is unconscionable. This contradicts the initial intention of having a single, consistent governing law and creates a scenario where South Carolina's conflict of law rules would need to be invoked.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a conflicting choice of law provision by specifying that Texas law governs trade secret ownership while the original text states that South Carolina law governs the entire agreement. This misalignment of terminology creates a legal contradiction as it deviates from a legally recognized definition of a unified governing law. The perturbation explanation also indicates that the Master Distributor is organized under Texas law, further emphasizing the conflict. The law explanation highlights the uncertainty and potential conflict introduced and how a court would need to determine whether this carve-out violates public policy or is unconscionable.

perturbation.original_text: 1. Definitions. (p) Territory. The term "Territory" means the world.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. Definitions. (p) Territory. The term "Territory" means Charleston County, South Carolina.
perturbation.explanation: This change limits the definition of "Territory" to only Charleston County, South Carolina. This directly affects the scope of the agreement, especially concerning trade secret protection and distribution rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Lanham Act, which governs trademark law at the federal level, inherently contemplates interstate and international commerce. By restricting the definition of "Territory" to a single county, the contract limits the potential reach of trademark protection and creates conflict with federal trademark law, especially if the Company's products or services are sold or marketed outside of Charleston County. The modification is in contradiction of the premise of the Lanham Act, which is for national protection.

perturbation.justification: The modification to the contract text limits the 'Territory' to Charleston County, South Carolina, conflicting with the Lanham Act's contemplation of interstate and international commerce. The law explanation states that this restriction limits the potential reach of trademark protection and contradicts federal trademark law, especially if the company's products or services are sold or marketed outside of Charleston County. Snippet 2, which lists sections of the Lanham Act, includes mentions of remedies for infringement, false designations of origin, international conventions, and definitions. These provisions suggest a broader scope than a single county, supporting the claim that the contract modification contradicts the intent and application of federal trademark law.

perturbation.original_text: Master Distributor shall not challenge, directly or indirectly, the right, title, and interest of Company in and to the Proprietary Information, Intellectual Property and Products, nor the validity or enforceability of Company's claimed rights therein under the laws of any nation or state within the Territory.
perturbation.changed_text: Master Distributor shall not challenge, directly or indirectly, the right, title, and interest of Company in and to the Proprietary Information, Intellectual Property and Products, regardless of their validity or enforceability.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the clause "under the laws of any nation or state within the Territory". This attempts to prevent challenges even if the company's claimed rights are not valid or enforceable under the relevant laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South Carolina Unfair Trade Practices Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text could be interpreted as restricting the Master Distributor's ability to challenge unfair trade practices by the Company, even if the Company's claims are invalid under the law. The South Carolina Unfair Trade Practices Act protects businesses from anti-competitive or deceptive practices. By removing the reference to the laws of any nation or state, the Master Distributor's ability to claim violation of fair trade laws is removed.

Quick Search Search for: Full Site Search Bill Number Legislation Budget Code of Laws Code of Regulations Constitution House Journals Senate Journals LSA Bill Summary Find Your Legislators Contact Your Legislator Legislation Track Legislation Multi-Criteria Search Research South Carolina Law Ratifications & Acts Code of Laws Code of Regulations Constitution State Register Legislative Manual Purchase Manual View Manual Online Student Connection Visiting the State House Archives State Agency Websites Media Links South Carolina Law > Code of Laws > Title 39 South Carolina Code of Laws Unannotated Title 39 - Trade and Commerce CHAPTER 5 Unfair Trade Practices ARTICLE 1 General Provisions SECTION 39-5-10. Short title; definitions. This article may be cited as the "South Carolina Unfair Trade Practices Act." As used in this article, (a) "Person" shall include natural persons, corporations, trusts, partnerships, incorporated or unincorporated associations and any other legal entity. (b) "Trade" and "commerce" shall include the advertising, offering for sale, sale or distribution of any services and any property, tangible or intangible, real, personal or mixed, and any other article, commodity or thing of value wherever situate, and shall include any trade or commerce directly or indirectly affecting the people of this State. (c) "Documentary material" shall include the original or a copy of any book, record, report, memorandum, paper, communication, tabulation, map, chart, photograph, mechanical transcription or other tangible document or recording, wherever situate. (d) "Examination" of documentary material shall include the inspection, study or copying of any such material, and the taking of testimony under oath or acknowledgment in respect of any such documentary material or copy thereof. HISTORY: 1962 Code Section 66-71; 1971 (57) 369. SECTION 39-5-20. Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices unlawful; application of federal act. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to Section 5(a) (1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. HISTORY: 1962 Code Section 66-71.1; 1971 (57) 369. SECTION 39-5-35. Requiring certain insurance coverage as unfair trade practice. It shall be an unlawful trade practice under Section 39-5-20 for any person engaged in the business of lending money to make it a condition of obtaining a loan for the purchase of an automobile that the borrower carry full coverage comprehensive or fifty dollars collision coverage. HISTORY: 1979 Act No. 121 Section 5. SECTION 39-5-36. Resale of tickets for more than original price. (A) A person or firm who knowingly purchases a quantity of tickets for admission to an event which exceeds the maximum quantity posted by or on behalf of the original ticket seller at the point of original sale or printed on the tickets and intends to resell the tickets in excess of one dollar above the price charged by the original ticket seller violates the South Carolina Unfair Trade Practices Act and is subject to its provisions, penalties, and damages. (B) A person or firm who violates the provisions of Section 16-17-710(A) is subject to the provisions, penalties, and damages of the South Carolina Unfair Trade Practices Act. (C) A person or firm is not liable pursuant to this section with respect to tickets for which the person or firm is the original ticket seller. (D) For purposes of this section, the term "original ticket seller" means the issuer of the tickets or a person or firm who provides tick

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the limitation that the Master Distributor can only challenge the Company's rights based on the laws of a nation or state within the territory. By removing "under the laws of any nation or state within the Territory," the Master Distributor is seemingly prevented from challenging the validity or enforceability of the Company's rights, even if those rights are invalid under applicable laws. This creates a contradiction because it could restrict the Master Distributor's ability to challenge unfair trade practices, even if the Company's claims are not legally sound. This restriction directly opposes the South Carolina Unfair Trade Practices Act, which declares unlawful "unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce" (Snippet 2). By hindering the Master Distributor's ability to challenge potentially unfair practices, the modified text contradicts the intent of the Act.
